style.name: Swiss International
style.philosophy: Grid is law. Type does the heavy lifting. Decoration is failure.

tokens.borders: hairline (1px), pure or near-black, used as separators only
tokens.shadows: none — depth comes from spacing, not light
tokens.type: Helvetica/Inter at strict ratios (1.2 or 1.25), generous tracking on caps
tokens.color: black on white, single accent at <60% saturation used 1-2% of surface area

style.notes: The trap is mistaking minimalism for blankness — Swiss is precise placement, not empty canvas.

type_pair.name: Young Serif × Inter × JetBrains Mono
type_pair.notes: Single-weight display means you cannot build a heading hierarchy from Young Serif alone — use size only.

stacks.framer_motion: { initial: { y: 12, opacity: 0 }, animate: { y: 0, opacity: 1 }, transition: { duration: 0.36, ease: [0.16, 1, 0.3, 1] } }
stacks.css: @keyframes fade-up-12 { from { transform: translateY(12px); opacity: 0; } to { transform: translateY(0); opacity: 1; } } .fade-up-12 { animation: fade-up-12 360ms cubic-bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1) both; }

motion.name: Fade-In Pure

stacks.framer_motion: { initial: { opacity: 0 }, animate: { opacity: 1 }, transition: { duration: 0.2, ease: [0.4, 0, 0.2, 1] } }
stacks.css: @keyframes fade-in { from { opacity: 0; } to { opacity: 1; } } .fade-in { animation: fade-in 200ms cubic-bezier(0.4, 0.0, 0.2, 1) both; }

motion.reduced_motion: no change — already opacity-only, safe as-is
motion.performance: opacity only — GPU composited, cheapest entry

motion.name: Fade-Up 24px Slow

stacks.framer_motion: { initial: { y: 24, opacity: 0 }, animate: { y: 0, opacity: 1 }, transition: { duration: 0.56, ease: [0.16, 1, 0.3, 1] } }
stacks.css: @keyframes fade-up-24 { from { transform: translateY(24px); opacity: 0; } to { transform: translateY(0); opacity: 1; } } .fade-up-24 { animation: fade-up-24 560ms cubic-bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1) both; }

motion.reduced_motion: instant — fade only at 200ms, no transform
motion.performance: transform + opacity only — GPU composited; use for hero-level reveals not list items

motion.name: Slide-Up From Clip

stacks.framer_motion: { initial: { y: '100%' }, animate: { y: 0 }, transition: { duration: 0.48, ease: [0.22, 1, 0.36, 1] } }
stacks.css: @keyframes slide-up-clip { from { transform: translateY(100%); } to { transform: translateY(0); } } .slide-up-clip-parent { overflow: hidden; } .slide-up-clip { animation: slide-up-clip 480ms cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1) both; }

motion.performance: transform only — GPU composited; requires parent with overflow:hidden for the mask effect

motion.name: Scale-Pop 95 to 100

stacks.framer_motion: { initial: { scale: 0.95, opacity: 0 }, animate: { scale: 1, opacity: 1 }, transition: { duration: 0.22, ease: [0.16, 1, 0.3, 1] } }
stacks.css: @keyframes scale-pop { from { transform: scale(0.95); opacity: 0; } to { transform: scale(1); opacity: 1; } } .scale-pop { animation: scale-pop 220ms cubic-bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1) both; transform-origin: center; }

motion.performance: transform + opacity only — GPU composited; default for popovers, menus, tooltips

components.name: Mobile Bottom Navigation
components.purpose: Five-or-fewer thumb-reachable destinations pinned to the bottom of mobile viewports.

components.name: Underline Tab Bar
components.purpose: Editorial-feeling tab navigation with a sliding underline indicator under the active label.

components.name: Searchable Select / Combobox
components.purpose: Dropdown select with type-ahead filtering, keyboard navigation, and groupable options.
a11y: Esc closes and restores prior value

components.name: Inline Date Picker
components.purpose: Calendar grid for selecting a single date with month/year navigation and keyboard arrows.
a11y: screen reader announces full date on focus

components.name: Magnetic Button
components.purpose: Button that subtly tracks the cursor within its bounding radius for premium hover feel.

components.name: Floating Action Button (Mobile)
components.purpose: Persistent circular action button fixed bottom-right for the primary screen action.
a11y: z-order does not obscure critical text

components.name: Tall Bento Card
components.purpose: Tall asymmetric card for bento-grid layouts, holding mixed image+text content.

components.name: Page Shell Loader
components.purpose: Full-page skeleton mimicking the upcoming layout (nav + content + sidebar) during initial load.
a11y: real layout matches skeleton (no jolt on load)

components.name: Calendar Heatmap
components.purpose: GitHub-style yearly grid where each day-cell intensity encodes activity volume.
a11y: each cell has aria-label with date and value

components.name: Notification Preferences Matrix
components.purpose: Grid of notification categories across delivery channels (email/push/SMS) with per-cell toggles.

components.name: Stat Card with Chart Background
components.purpose: Stat card where the entire background is a low-opacity sparkline or area chart of the metric.
a11y: chart marked aria-hidden (decorative); text values are primary data
contrast verified for text over chart

components.name: Typing Presence Indicator
components.purpose: Three-dot animated indicator showing another user is typing, with name and avatar.

brand_exemplars.name: Aesop
brand_exemplars.philosophy: The deepest apothecary in beauty — sepia canvas, chocolate ink, Walbaum serif everywhere, text-link CTAs instead of buttons. Reads like a 19th-century pharmacopeia.

design_language.motion_signature: None on hover. The brand voice is stillness — animation is anti-aesthetic. Quiet 200ms link fades only.

trademark_signals: Sepia canvas — the entire industry runs white, Aesop refuses
Walbaum serif everywhere — display, body, navigation, captions
Text-link CTAs instead of button-fill — absence of buttons is the brand
Latin botanical names italicized in ingredient lists — scientific cadence
Numbered instructional steps with serif numerals, not symbols
ai_slop_avoided: Don't render filled-button CTAs except in checkout
Don't introduce saturated colors — sepia is the entire palette
Don't animate beyond quiet 200ms link fades

brand_exemplars.name: Airbnb
brand_exemplars.philosophy: A warm, generous consumer marketplace anchored on a clean white canvas and Airbnb Rausch (#ff385c), the single brand voltage that carries every primary CTA, search-button orb, and rating dot.
trademark_signals: Single accent color: (#ff385c — "Rausch") carries every primary CTA, the search orb, the heart save state, and the brand wordmark. Used scarcely — most pages are 90% white + ink with one or two Rausch moments
Custom variable type: Airbnb Cereal VF. Display weights sit at 500–700, body at 400. Modest weight is intentional — the system trusts photography for visual heft
Three-product top nav: Homes, Experiences, Services — each with a hand-illustrated 32px icon and "NEW" badges on the two newer products. Active tab uses an underline rule
Pill-shaped global search bar: white surface, fully rounded, divided by 1px hairlines into Where / When / Who segments, terminated by a circular Rausch search orb
Property cards are photo-first: aspect-ratio rectangles with corner clipping, swipeable image carousel, "Guest favorite" floating badge top-left, heart icon top-right, then 4–5 lines of meta beneath

brand_exemplars.name: Airtable
brand_exemplars.philosophy: A sober, editorial workflow-software interface anchored on white canvas and dark-ink type, where brand voltage comes from full-bleed signature cards in coral, dark green, peach, and dark navy that punctuate long-scroll explainer pages.
trademark_signals: Primary CTA is (near-black ink) with white text and a (12px) corner — it reads as confident and final, never decorative
Secondary CTA is a button with text and a hairline outline. The two together form Airtable's signature button pair
Hero is white canvas. There is no atmospheric gradient, no mesh, no background flourish. The brand strength comes from the type and the buttons sitting in clean whitespace
Brand voltage lives in signature surface cards:, and carry full-bleed product callouts every few screens
Demo-card grids carry product UI fragments on, and other warm pastel surfaces
ai_slop_avoided: Don't make (#1b61c9) the primary button color. It is the link color. The primary button is (#181d26, near-black). Treating link-blue as the brand action is the most common mistake when reading Airtable's CSS variables
Don't add a gradient backdrop to the hero. Airtable's hero is white, full stop. Mesh, aurora, spotlight gradients all read as "another SaaS template"
Don't bold display-weight type. and are intentionally weight 400 / 500

brand_exemplars.name: Allbirds
brand_exemplars.philosophy: Warm-minimalism e-commerce — ivory canvas, sage and terracotta worn-material accents, serif-display literary voice, product photography on textured paper.

design_language.motion_signature: 180ms ease-out hover; signature 600ms slow scale-down on product photo hover; slide-up add-to-bag confirmation, no modal.

trademark_signals: Carbon footprint number displayed on every product like a price — serif numerals
Material-name eyebrow caps (Tree, Wool, SugarCane) — signature taxonomy
Product photography on textured/paper backgrounds, never plain white
Serif headlines in apparel — most footwear is geometric sans
Sage-bone surface for filter bands — never pure white
ai_slop_avoided: Don't add countdown timers or sale-urgency banners
Don't render product 3D rotations or AR chrome on PDP
Don't introduce saturated brand colors — every accent reads worn

brand_exemplars.name: Apple
brand_exemplars.philosophy: A photography-first interface that turns marketing into a museum gallery.
trademark_signals: Photography-first presentation; UI recedes so the product can speak
Alternating full-bleed tile sections: white/parchment ↔ near-black, with the color change itself acting as the section divider
Single blue accent (#0066cc) carries every interactive element. No second brand color exists
Two button grammars: tiny blue pill CTAs and compact utility rects
SF Pro Display + SF Pro Text — negative letter-spacing at display sizes for the signature "Apple tight" headline feel
ai_slop_avoided: Don't introduce a second accent color; every "click me" signal is (Action Blue)
Don't add shadows to cards, buttons, or text
Don't use gradients as decorative backgrounds; atmosphere comes from photography

guardrails.name: Inter used as display font
guardrails.why: Inter is a body font tuned for screen legibility at small sizes; deployed as display it reads as the default startup-landing fingerprint.
guardrails.fix: Pair Inter (body) with a distinctive display face: Geist, Satoshi, Cabinet Grotesk, General Sans, Outfit, or a brand-specific variable sans.

guardrails.name: Default purple-to-blue AI gradient
guardrails.why: Purple-to-blue gradient on white is the strongest visual fingerprint of unconstrained model output and reads as template-marketplace AI slop.
guardrails.fix: Use a single restrained accent (Emerald, Electric Blue, Deep Rose, Amber) against neutrals; keep gradient hue spread under 60 degrees if used at all.

guardrails.name: Three equal cards in a row
guardrails.why: Three equal cards with three icons and three short paragraphs is the safest default the generator reaches for and the strongest layout fingerprint in AI-generated marketing surfaces.
guardrails.fix: Use asymmetric layouts: bento grids, 2-and-1 splits, 4 with one spanning width. Vary card size by content priority.

guardrails.name: Generic placeholder names
guardrails.why: John/Jane Doe and their cousins signal that nobody thought about who would actually use the product - immediate AI-generated-tutorial vibe.
guardrails.fix: Use plausible names that fit the target market: Maya Iqbal, Adam Levin, Wen Zhang, Layla Haddad. Match region for regional products.

guardrails.name: Lorem ipsum in shipping code
guardrails.why: Lorem ipsum in production source code is unshipped placeholder content - the most obvious draft-state leak.
guardrails.fix: Write real copy that makes a specific claim about the product. If you can't write it yet, mark the block with TODO and a content owner.

guardrails.name: Emoji used as UI element
guardrails.why: Emojis render inconsistently across platforms, ignore brand color, and signal informality where SVG icons signal craft.
guardrails.fix: Inline SVG from Lucide, Feather, Phosphor, or Heroicons at 1.5-2px stroke with currentColor. Whitelist U+2713 check and U+2318 command only.

guardrails.name: Centered hero composition
guardrails.why: Centered headline + centered subtitle + centered button stack is the laziest hero composition and signals the generator picked it when it couldn't find a better layout.
guardrails.fix: Use left-aligned hero with editorial 7-5 or 8-4 grid split; let the imagery earn its own column.

guardrails.name: Multi-stop gradient text
guardrails.why: Three-stop rainbow text on a hero word is the strongest 'AI hero' tell after the purple-to-blue gradient.
guardrails.fix: Use a single solid color for headlines. If gradient text is essential, two stops in analogous hues at modest saturation.

guardrails.name: Purple glow shadow on cards
guardrails.why: Purple-tinted shadows on cards are the second-strongest 'AI premium' fingerprint after the gradient itself.
guardrails.fix: Use neutral diffusion shadow: shadow-[0_20px_40px_-15px_rgba(0,0,0,0.08)]. Color comes from the surface, not the shadow.

guardrails.name: Default 300ms transition timing
guardrails.why: 300ms is the editor default - using it everywhere signals zero motion intent and reads as laziness to anyone who tunes animation curves.
guardrails.fix: Micro-interactions 150-220ms, complex transitions 250-400ms, exit 60-70% of entry. Pick durations that match the feel, not the default.

guardrails.name: Material default easing everywhere
guardrails.why: cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1) is Material Design's default standard ease - using it as the only curve signals no taste decision was made.
guardrails.fix: Use cubic-bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1) for premium exits, cubic-bezier(0.34, 1.56, 0.64, 1) for playful overshoot, or spring physics.

guardrails.name: System font stack with no chosen typeface
guardrails.why: Falling back to the system stack alone with no chosen typeface gives up the chance to have any typographic identity.
guardrails.fix: Pick a real face: Geist, Inter, Satoshi, IBM Plex Sans, Cabinet Grotesk, or the brand's variable. System stack is the absolute last fallback.

guardrails.name: Emoji used as icon stamp
guardrails.why: Emoji where an SVG icon belongs breaks brand color, scales poorly, and renders differently on every OS.
guardrails.fix: Inline SVG icon with currentColor and a consistent stroke width across the surface.

guardrails.name: rounded-full applied to everything
guardrails.why: rounded-full on every button and input is the iOS-tutorial fingerprint - signals the radius decision was skipped entirely.
guardrails.fix: rounded-full for pills, avatars, icon buttons only. Use rounded-md or rounded-lg for primary buttons; rounded-xl or rounded-2xl for cards.

guardrails.name: Arbitrary hero font size
guardrails.why: Arbitrary px values like text-[90px] or text-[112px] bypass the type scale and signal the size was picked by eye, not by system.
guardrails.fix: Extend the Tailwind theme scale or use clamp() for fluid hero typography. text-6xl/text-7xl/text-8xl exist for a reason.

guardrails.name: Backdrop blur with no glass surface
guardrails.why: Backdrop blur applied without a translucent surface behind it is GPU work for zero visual benefit - decoration, not design.
guardrails.fix: Pair backdrop-blur with a translucent fill: bg-white/70 or bg-zinc-950/60. Otherwise remove the blur entirely.

guardrails.name: Generic overlapping avatar stack
guardrails.why: Three overlapping circular avatars in the nav as 'our team' or 'join 10k users' is a content-marketing template tell.
guardrails.fix: Use real customer logos, named testimonials with quotes, or specific signal (Used by 312 engineering teams at Series-A companies).

guardrails.name: Hardcoded five-star testimonial
guardrails.why: Five hardcoded stars next to a fake quote is the lowest-trust pattern on the web; users have learned to discount it on sight.
guardrails.fix: Show real, named, attributable testimonials with company affiliation and a specific outcome. No star count unless aggregated from a real review system.

guardrails.name: Bouncing arrow on CTA
guardrails.why: Bouncing arrow on a CTA is the desperate-attention pattern that signals the copy itself isn't doing the work.
guardrails.fix: Let the CTA copy carry the action (Start a 14-day trial, Read the deployment guide). If you need an arrow, use a static SVG that nudges 2-4px on hover.

guardrails.name: Hamburger menu on desktop
guardrails.why: Hamburger on a wide viewport hides nav from users for no reason; signals the responsive breakpoint was skipped.
guardrails.fix: Show inline nav on md: and up; hide the hamburger above that breakpoint with md:hidden.

guardrails.name: Named Tailwind colors with no semantic token
guardrails.why: Raw bg-blue-500 / bg-purple-500 with no semantic token (primary, success, danger) signals the color system was never designed.
guardrails.fix: Define semantic tokens in tailwind.config or CSS custom properties: bg-primary, text-success, ring-accent. Map them once.

guardrails.name: Inline style attribute
guardrails.why: Inline style= attributes bypass the design system, defeat caching, and signal the styling decision was made ad hoc.
guardrails.fix: Use Tailwind utilities, a CSS class, or a styled component. Reserve inline style for runtime-computed values (animated translate, dynamic CSS vars) only.

guardrails.name: console.log in component code
guardrails.why: console.log shipped to production leaks state to anyone with DevTools open and signals the build didn't go through a real review.
guardrails.fix: Remove debug logs before shipping. If you need structured logging, use a logger module that's tree-shaken out of production builds.

guardrails.name: TODO or FIXME in shipping code
guardrails.why: TODO/FIXME in shipping code is an unresolved promise to the reader and a draft-state leak.
guardrails.fix: Resolve the TODO before merge, or move it to the issue tracker with a specific owner and date.

guardrails.name: TypeScript any type
guardrails.why: any defeats the type system and signals the type was never figured out - the code is JavaScript pretending to be TypeScript.
guardrails.fix: Use unknown for genuinely unknown shapes and narrow with type guards. Use a specific interface or generic for everything else.

guardrails.name: SVG without aria-label or aria-hidden
guardrails.why: SVG with no aria-label and no aria-hidden is announced by screen readers as 'graphic' with no context - failure of WCAG 1.1.1.
guardrails.fix: Decorative SVG gets aria-hidden="true". Informative SVG gets aria-label="description" or aria-labelledby pointing to a title element.

guardrails.name: Button missing type attribute
guardrails.why: A <button> inside a <form> without type="button" defaults to type="submit" and silently submits the form on every click.
guardrails.fix: Always set type="button" or type="submit" explicitly. The default is a footgun, not a feature.

guardrails.name: Image missing alt attribute
guardrails.why: Missing alt breaks screen readers, SEO image indexing, and the broken-image fallback all at once - WCAG 1.1.1 failure.
guardrails.fix: Every gets alt. Decorative images get alt="" (empty). Informative images get a description a screen reader user would need.

guardrails.name: Anchor with onClick but no href
guardrails.why: An <a> without href is not focusable by default and is invisible to assistive tech - it looks like a link but behaves like a button.
guardrails.fix: Use <button> for actions. Use <a href="..."> for navigation. Never an anchor without href as a click target.

guardrails.name: Skipped heading level
guardrails.why: Heading hierarchy gaps (h1 then h3, or h3 with no h1/h2 preceding) break the screen-reader landmark tree and the SEO outline.
guardrails.fix:
h1 once per page; nest h2/h3/h4 in order. If you need a small visual heading without a logical level, use a styled

instead.

guardrails.name: Default shadcn token block unmodified
guardrails.why: The default shadcn HSL token block with --radius 0.5rem is recognizable in two seconds by anyone who has shipped one shadcn app.
guardrails.fix: Customize tokens at init: swap slate for zinc/stone, shift primary to a brand hue, set --radius to 0.625rem or 0.75rem.

guardrails.name: Purple-pink mesh gradient hero
guardrails.why: Purple-to-pink mesh gradient is the second-most fingerprinted AI-hero pattern after blue-to-purple - 'I generated a landing page' aesthetic.
guardrails.fix: Single accent against neutral canvas. If a hero needs depth, layer subtle noise or a single low-saturation analogous gradient under 60deg hue spread.

guardrails.name: Low-contrast text on card
guardrails.why: Dark text token (zinc-500/600/700) on a dark surface fails WCAG AA 4.5:1 contrast and signals the dark mode pairing was never tested.
guardrails.fix: Test contrast on both themes. On dark surfaces use text-zinc-100 to text-zinc-300; reserve 500-700 for tertiary captions on light surfaces.

guardrails.name: Lazy z-index value
guardrails.why: z-index: 9999 is the 'I gave up on the stacking system' value - signals there's no z-scale token map and stacking bugs are coming.
guardrails.fix: Define a z-scale token set (z-base: 0, z-dropdown: 10, z-sticky: 20, z-modal: 50, z-toast: 60). Use the tokens, never a raw 9999.

guardrails.name: Infinite scroll without keyboard fallback
guardrails.why: Pure infinite scroll with no Load more button locks out keyboard users and breaks back-button history - WCAG 2.4 failure plus a usability one.
guardrails.fix: Ship infinite scroll AND a visible Load more button. The button is the keyboard path; the observer is the convenience layer.

guardrails.name: Viewport blocks pinch-zoom
guardrails.why: user-scalable=no blocks pinch-zoom for low-vision users on touch devices - a WCAG 1.4.4 failure that locks out anyone who needs to enlarge text.
guardrails.fix: Remove user-scalable=no entirely, or set it to yes. Default viewport: <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">.

guardrails.name: outline removed without focus-visible replacement
guardrails.why: outline: none removes the only keyboard focus indicator and leaves keyboard users with no signal of focus state - a WCAG 2.4.7 failure.
guardrails.fix: Pair outline: none with a :focus-visible rule that restores a visible ring: :focus-visible { outline: 2px solid var(--primary); outline-offset: 2px; }.

guardrails.name: Input placeholder used as the only label
guardrails.why: Placeholders disappear on focus, sit at low contrast by default, and are not announced consistently by assistive tech - using one as the only label fails WCAG 3.3.2.
guardrails.fix: Always pair <input> with a <label for="id"> or aria-label. Placeholder is for an optional hint, never the field name.

guardrails.name: Click handler on <div> without role or tabindex
guardrails.why: A <div onClick> is invisible to keyboard users and assistive tech - the action exists only for mouse users, a WCAG 2.1.1 and 4.1.2 failure.
guardrails.fix: Use <button onClick> for actions. If you must keep the <div>, add role="button" tabindex="0" and an onKeyDown handler for Enter and Space.

guardrails.name: Anchor styled as button without href
guardrails.why: An <a class="btn"> without href is not focusable, not in the tab order, and not announced as a link - looks like a button but behaves like nothing.
guardrails.fix: Use <button> for actions, <a href="..."> for navigation. If the destination is unknown until runtime, render a button or set href="#" with preventDefault.

guardrails.name: 100vh without 100dvh fallback for mobile
guardrails.why: 100vh on mobile includes the browser chrome that retracts on scroll, so the layout jumps as the address bar collapses - dynamic viewport units (dvh) fix this.
guardrails.fix: Use height: 100dvh or h-[100dvh] as the primary, with h-screen / 100vh as the legacy fallback for browsers that lack dvh.

guardrails.name: Title Case On Display Headings
guardrails.why: Marketing-style Title Case on every headline dates the design to a 2014 SaaS template and slows reading. Sentence case scales better and reads faster.
guardrails.fix: Default to sentence case for headlines. Reserve title case for proper nouns and brand names, or for an explicit editorial decision.

guardrails.name: Filler marketing verb in headline
guardrails.why: Elevate, Seamless, Unleash, Revolutionize, Empower, Supercharge, Transform - the unmistakable fingerprint of unedited marketing copy generated without a real product in mind.
guardrails.fix: Name the specific action and the specific outcome. Cut the verb if it isn't carrying weight. The reader knows what the product does only if you say what the product does.

guardrails.name: Generic CTA text (Click here, Learn more)
guardrails.why: Click here / Learn more / Get started reveals nothing about the destination and fails screen-reader scan-by-link - users hear a list of identical labels with no context.
guardrails.fix: Name the specific action: Read pricing, Start free trial, View dashboard, Open API docs. The link text should make sense out of context.

guardrails.name: Round number marketing stat
guardrails.why: 99.99%, 10x faster, 100% guaranteed - the suspiciously round numbers AI invents to sound credible. Real measurements are messy and specific.
guardrails.fix: Use the real number you measured: 99.93% uptime over Q1, 4.2x faster median build, refunded 218 of 244 reported issues. Specificity is the signal.

guardrails.name: Display heading at 700+ font-weight
guardrails.why: Display fonts at 700 weight read dense and visually muddy. Most display faces are drawn to sing at 500-600; 700+ is for body emphasis, not headlines.
guardrails.fix: Use font-weight 500 or 600 on display headings. Reserve 700+ for body emphasis (bold inline text, table totals, primary CTA labels).

guardrails.name: ALL CAPS at body-text size or larger
guardrails.why: ALL CAPS reading speed is 13-20% slower than mixed case because word shape is lost. At 14px or larger it actively impedes reading.
guardrails.fix: Reserve uppercase for eyebrows, labels, and badges under 14px. Use sentence case for anything readers need to read, not just scan.

guardrails.name: cursor:pointer on non-interactive element
guardrails.why: A <div> or <span> with cursor: pointer promises clickable behavior to sighted mouse users while keyboard and screen-reader users get nothing - a hidden interaction.
guardrails.fix: If it clicks, make it a <button> or <a href>. If you keep the <div>, add role and tabindex and a keydown handler - the cursor change is the smallest signal a real control owes.

guardrails.name: Transition on a layout property
guardrails.why: Animating width, height, top, left, margin, padding triggers layout and paint on every frame - the dropped-frame jank pattern that makes the UI feel cheap.
guardrails.fix: Animate transform (translate, scale) and opacity only. Use scale instead of width, translate instead of top/left. The compositor thanks you.

guardrails.name: Image without width and height attributes
guardrails.why: An without width and height attributes causes Cumulative Layout Shift as the image loads and the page reflows - the single biggest CLS regression on most sites.
guardrails.fix: Always set width and height (the intrinsic pixel dimensions) on every , even when styled with CSS. The browser uses them to reserve space.

guardrails.name: Lone emoji used as functional icon
guardrails.why: A bare emoji standing in for an icon renders inconsistently across platforms, ignores brand color, and is announced unpredictably by screen readers.
guardrails.fix: Use inline SVG (Lucide, Feather, Phosphor, Heroicons) at 1.5-2px stroke with currentColor. Add aria-label when the icon carries meaning.

guardrails.name: Chrome-y multi-stop gradient
guardrails.why: Three-plus color stops in a linear-gradient is the v0 / Cursor / generic-AI default; reads as chrome-y template output rather than a deliberate brand decision.
guardrails.fix: Use a two-stop gradient (or a single solid) with hue spread under 60 degrees. If depth is needed, layer a low-opacity overlay instead of stacking stops.

guardrails.name: Five-plus box-shadow layers stacked
guardrails.why: Stacking five or more shadow layers is the AI's idea of premium depth; reads as visual bloat and chews the GPU on paint.
guardrails.fix: Use one ambient diffusion shadow (e.g. 0 20px 40px -15px rgba(0,0,0,0.08)) and at most one tight contact shadow. Depth comes from contrast, not layer count.

guardrails.name: Pixel height on a text container
guardrails.why: Fixing a pixel height on a container that also styles text breaks reflow at every other zoom level and font scale - the layout is one breakpoint away from clipping.
guardrails.fix: Use min-height for floor protection, line-height + padding for vertical rhythm, and let content determine height. Never lock text height in px.

guardrails.name: Over-the-top hover scale
guardrails.why: scale(1.1) or higher on hover reads as amateur - it shouts when a 1-2 percent nudge or a shadow lift would be more confident.
guardrails.fix: Use scale(1.01) to scale(1.03), or replace the scale with a translateY(-2px) plus shadow shift. Restraint reads premium.

guardrails.name: Dynamic Island inner glow on cards
guardrails.why: iOS Dynamic Island's inner-glow + blur recipe is being copied as the default on every card and pill - reads as the 2024 AI-marketing-template tell.
guardrails.fix: If you want depth, use a single outer diffusion shadow tuned to the surface. Reserve inset glows for a deliberate metallic or pill-style accent, not every surface.

guardrails.name: backdrop-filter blur without background fallback
guardrails.why: backdrop-filter is unsupported on Firefox and older Safari - without a translucent background fallback, the surface renders fully transparent and content underneath bleeds through.
guardrails.fix: Always pair backdrop-filter: blur with a background-color (e.g. background: rgba(255,255,255,0.7)). The bg is the fallback; the blur is the upgrade.

guardrails.name: Default AI pricing tier amounts
guardrails.why: $9 / $19 / $29 / $49 / $99 / $199 are the prices AI reaches for when no real pricing exists - unconvincing placeholders that signal nobody priced the product.
guardrails.fix: Use the real price you charge, with the real currency and the real billing cadence. If pricing isn't set yet, mark the tier as TBD with an owner, not a placeholder.

guardrails.name: Unsourced trust-by claim
guardrails.why: Trusted by 1000+ teams / Used by Fortune 500 / Loved by 50k developers - unsourced trust claims that signal the generator invented the number to fill space.
guardrails.fix: Name the actual companies, link to the actual case studies, or show the actual review aggregate. Specificity beats magnitude every time.

guardrails.name: Fake just-now timestamp in static markup
guardrails.why: Just now / moments ago in static HTML is a fake activity signal - it stays Just now forever and signals nobody wired the real time-ago helper.
guardrails.fix: Render real timestamps via a time-ago helper bound to a real source (Date.now, server time, last_event_at). If the surface is static, use a real date string instead.

guardrails.name: Rainbow gradient on display text
guardrails.why: background-clip: text on a multi-stop linear-gradient is the AI hero's chrome-y rainbow word - the loudest signal that no taste decision was made on color.
guardrails.fix: Use one solid color on display text. If gradient text is essential, two stops in analogous hues at modest saturation, never three-plus rainbow stops.

guardrails.name: Emoji at start of list item
guardrails.why: An emoji at the start of every list item is the AI signature - check marks, sparkles, rockets, party poppers used as bullet markers signal a generator that confused decoration with hierarchy.
guardrails.fix: Use the native list disc, an SVG check icon, or a numeric counter. Reserve the U+2713 check and U+2318 command as allowed dingbats.

guardrails.name: Animated grain or noise without GPU hint
guardrails.why: Animated grain or noise without a will-change or transform hint forces a CPU repaint every frame and causes paint thrashing on low-end devices.
guardrails.fix: Add will-change: transform or compose the noise via an SVG <feTurbulence> overlay on a transformed layer. Or drop the animation - static grain reads the same and costs nothing.

guardrails.name: Unsourced large-number marketing claim
guardrails.why: We saved 10,000 hours / Generated 1,000,000 lines of code / Reduced 50,000 tickets - large round numbers with no citation that signal the magnitude was invented to sound impressive.
guardrails.fix: Use the real measured number with the real source: 'In Q1 2026, customers saved 4,217 support hours (mean across 38 teams)'. Real numbers are messy and specific.

guardrails.name: Magnitude-default before-after claim
guardrails.why: 0 to 100 / 100% guaranteed / 100% effective are the magnitude-by-default phrases AI reaches for - readers have learned to discount them.
guardrails.fix: Quote the real spread: 'from 14 manual steps to 3 automated'. If you can't measure it, don't claim it.

guardrails.name: Logo cloud image with empty alt
guardrails.why: An inside a Trusted by / logo-cloud section with alt="" is an unsourced trust claim with no machine-readable company name - the logo is decorative even when the surface is selling credibility.
guardrails.fix: Set alt to the real company name (alt="Stripe"), and only use the logo if you have permission. If you don't have permission, you don't have the trust signal.

guardrails.name: Load-bearing copy hidden in a title attribute
guardrails.why: A title attribute carrying 60+ characters of real content is hover-only - invisible on touch, inconsistently announced by screen readers, undiscoverable on keyboard. The information is effectively missing for most users.
guardrails.fix: Move the content into the visible flow or into an aria-describedby popover that is reachable by keyboard and touch. Reserve title for one-line, optional clarifications.

guardrails.name: Fixed scroll-to-top button on short pages
guardrails.why: A floating back-to-top button on a page under four viewports adds permanent visual noise for a vanishingly rare interaction - signals the generator included the pattern as decoration, not need.
guardrails.fix: Drop the button. If pages can grow past four viewports, gate the button on scroll position and hide it below threshold. Keyboard users have Home; mouse users have the wheel.

rationale: Type pair: Young Serif × Inter × JetBrains Mono
